channel.title: Gina Martiny — maniginam.dev
channel.description: Software craftsmanship, responsible AI, leadership lessons from chemical plants, and the messy reality of building things that work.

item.title: The Gauge Said Everything Was Fine
item.description: Surveys say 76% of small businesses use AI. Production data says 17%. The gap between what the dial reads and what's actually moving through the pipe is the whole story of 2026.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Twenty Kills Per Second
item.description: My oldest came skidding around the corner in socks on hardwood — eyes wide, voice cracking with the particular urgency that only an eight-year-old whose Minecraft world is under siege can produce.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: The Board Doesn't Lie
item.description: Every morning at 6:15, before the coffee kicked in, we stood in front of the board. Five letters. Big. Color-coded. Red or green.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Build It Once
item.description: Your MVP has a procurement process to survive. A security review. A compliance checklist. A VP who put their name on the approval. It has to live in that world from day one.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Bland Confidence
item.description: One of our AI agents rewrote a function, ran the tests, watched them pass, and shipped it. Except the function was wrong.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: We Build Software with Responsible AI
item.description: Teams are building AI features with none of the discipline they'd apply to literally any other part of their system.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: The Hive is Open
item.description: I was sitting at my desk with six AI agents running in six terminal panes, and I couldn't tell what any of them were doing.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: I Made Some Mistakes. The Robot Made Them Faster.
item.description: Last week I committed a Secure cookie flag to production that only worked on localhost. The AI helped with all of it. Enthusiastically.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Heat Disappearing Ink is Dangerous...
item.description: I decided to save my original EPIC drawings by laminating them, only to remember I'd drawn them with a heat-sensitive erasable pen.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Navigating the Unknown and Avoiding the Money Pit
item.description: Spending ten years in chemical manufacturing is not the typical path to preparing for a career in software development. But that's what I did.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: EPIC Growth
item.description: From a toddler's one-word school report to building a full production platform — a reflection on growth, persistence, and the slow accumulation of craft.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Don't Get Stranded Testlessly
item.description: On the importance of testing and what happens when you skip it — told through the lens of learning TDD from scratch.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Car Soup is Too Hot
item.description: Life lessons from toddlers, applied to software development. Sometimes the simplest observations cut the deepest.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Milk is in a Cup
item.description: Observations from raising kids while learning to code — where the mundane becomes metaphor.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Pee-Yoop!
item.description: The apprenticeship continues. Learning, failing, growing — with colorful commentary from the peanut gallery at home.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Stuck and Growing Fast
item.description: That particular feeling when you're stuck on a problem and growing at the same time. The apprenticeship grind, unvarnished.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: The Start of Two Recurs
item.description: Recursion, explained — twice. When code calls itself and toddlers repeat themselves, the parallels are uncanny.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Cheeky Butt
item.description: Kids say the darndest things. So do error messages. Reflections from the intersection of parenting and programming.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Refactor, Refactor, Drop!
item.description: Learning to refactor code while learning to let go. The apprenticeship diaries continue.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Cats VS HTTP
item.description: HTTP status codes explained through the universal language of cats. Because sometimes the best way to learn protocols is through memes.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Roadmaps
item.description: Planning the path forward — apprenticeship roadmaps, learning plans, and figuring out what to tackle next.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Choo Choo Peanut Poop
item.description: The title says it all. Parenthood meets apprenticeship in the most unglamorous way possible.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Dragon Drops
item.description: Drag and drop, explained. Also dragons. The early apprenticeship blog entries where code concepts meet creative storytelling.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: Eleventeen
item.description: The very beginning. Starting a software apprenticeship with two small kids, a chemical engineering background, and zero idea what I was getting into.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny

item.title: A Beginner's Path
item.description: I wanted to learn how to code, but I neither knew where to start or what that path forward might look like. A decade of false starts before finding the right approach.
item.dc:creator: Gina Martiny
